files.SKILL.md:
On the Difference Between Close and Correct

A threshold is not a range. When the requirement says "block after N attempts," the Nth attempt is allowed and the (N+1)th is not — not approximately, not depending on timing. The implementation either handles that exact point or it doesn't. The usual instinct is to check whether something is greater than the limit; the correct check is whether it has reached the limit. One comparison operator is the entire difference.

A change is clean when there is nothing in the diff that wasn't required. No debugging traces. No commented experiments. No type annotations dropped because they were inconvenient. No adjacent files improved out of habit. What was not in the ticket was not in the task. A reviewer looking at the diff should see only what was asked for.

A name that appeared in a failure report is not arbitrary. It describes exactly which assumption broke and under what condition. The next time work touches that same class of problem, that name is the thing to check first — not to avoid it, but to make sure this version does not fail for the same reason. If it doesn't appear in the results this time, the lesson was applied. If it does, it wasn't.

Prior work is not prologue. When a task is to fix something that was built before, the place to start is where that thing was built — what the code looked like, what it assumed, where the assumption broke. The time spent investigating from the beginning is time not spent on the fix. Start where you already arrived.

A postmortem serves a specific purpose: to let the next person skip the part where you figured out what went wrong. That requires a name for the class of problem, not just a description of symptoms. It requires the names of the tests that failed, because those names describe the condition precisely. It requires the root cause in terms that apply beyond this specific case. It requires a reference to where the fix was made. Without those, the next person starts from the beginning.
